segments.title: History as institutional memory for officers
segments.summary: Why military and naval education needs historical study when modern courses and recent experience cannot preserve every operational lesson.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke argues that history supplies institutional memory that a short officer course cannot carry by itself. He traces the pressure on modern curricula to the shortened training courses introduced during the Second World War, where adding a subject can seem to require removing another. The problem becomes acute when recent operational experience is narrow. Postwar amphibious experience, he says, is largely experience of raids and precision strikes rather than major assaults. Historians therefore preserve the cases that officers may need to understand when no living organization can retain routine experience of operations at a larger scale.

evidence.note: The lecture connects shortened officer courses, postwar raiding experience, amphibious warfare, and institutional memory.

segments.title: Staff rides turn historical cases into amphibious planning questions
segments.summary: Why staff rides complement historians' books by turning historical cases into practical questions about whether a battlefield or beach can support the force and logistics of a real operation.
segments.body: Books transmit historical lessons to officers who learn by reading, but Dr. Clarke stresses that many officers are doers who learn by doing. A staff ride brings historians together with veterans where available and serving officers who know the subject, then takes trainees through the ground involved. The aim is not merely to recount a battle but to give officers a close practical encounter with the choices it imposed. For an amphibious landing, the video turns that encounter into questions about beach suitability, space behind the beach, and the capacity to marshal logistics. A beach may accommodate one brigade yet still form a dangerous bottleneck for a major force. The Falklands War and Second World War operations illustrate why officers need historical comparisons when contemporary exercises usually simulate only a much smaller landing because full-scale practice is expensive.

evidence.note: The lecture contrasts reading with learning by doing, defines staff rides, and applies them to landing space, logistics, bottlenecks, the Falklands, and large-scale amphibious operations.
